title: Guy L. Lenentine (1893–1985): A Border Life Between New Brunswick and Aroostook County
narrative: Guy L. Lenentine was born on 5 November 1893 in Canada, in the New Brunswick borderlands from which his family came. He belonged to the Lenentine family of York County, New Brunswick, a kindred whose name and roots run back into the rural settlements along the Saint John River valley. His father, Wilmot Randolph Lenentine, was born in 1858 in Canterbury, York County, New Brunswick, and the wider family was concentrated around Canterbury and Southampton, where a burial ground still bears the family name (the New Lenentine Cemetery in the Southampton district). Guy's mother is recorded in our family data as Parmelia ("Parm-lia") Gass. The Lenentines were among the many New Brunswick families whose lives straddled the international line, with one branch remaining in Canada and another putting down roots just across the border in Maine.

The origin of the Lenentine surname itself is genuinely uncertain, and the family's own oral tradition that it is an anglicization of a French or Acadian name should be treated as plausible but unproven. Several popular genealogy sites describe Lenentine as a French or Huguenot name brought from France, while a surname-distribution reference candidly states that its meaning is simply "not listed." What is clear from the records is that the name is rare, that it is most heavily concentrated today in Maine and in Canada, and that its documented home base is York County, New Brunswick. The exact path from an original French spelling to the modern "Lenentine" has not been verified here, so the precise etymology is best left an open question rather than stated with false confidence.

Guy came of age during the great cross-border movement that shaped Aroostook County. His father Wilmot crossed from Canterbury, New Brunswick, into Maine and died in 1933 in Monticello, Aroostook County—a small farming town just inside the United States, a short distance south of Houlton. Houlton, the shire town of Aroostook County, sits only about three miles from the New Brunswick boundary, and for families like the Lenentines the border was less a barrier than a seam stitching together one community of farms, churches, and markets. It was in this world that Guy made his life, married, and raised a family. His wife was Marguerite Lenentine; among their children was a daughter, Grace, born on 12 May 1920 in Monticello, who later attended Ricker Classical Institute in Houlton.

The Aroostook County of Guy's lifetime was, above all, potato country. The arrival of the railroad in the late nineteenth century turned Houlton and its neighboring towns into shipping hubs that sent the county's potato crop to distant markets, and the rhythm of planting, harvest, and the autumn "potato break" from school governed daily life across the region. While Guy's specific occupation is not documented in the free sources consulted here, he lived squarely within this agricultural economy, in the belt of Monticello, Hodgdon, and Houlton farming communities where the Lenentine name was well established. The family's enduring presence in the area is reflected in the long-running Lenentine reunion held on Labor Day weekend at the Monticello Grange Hall.

Guy's years spanned an extraordinary stretch of history, from 1893 to 1985—from the era of horse-drawn farm work and steam railroads through two world wars (Houlton itself hosted an Army air base and a prisoner-of-war camp during the Second World War), the rise of the automobile, and into the late twentieth century. Across those nine decades the border town remained his family's anchor, even as relatives scattered across New England. One member of the wider family, Charlotte Lenentine, became a local historian and helped write the bicentennial history of the Houlton area for the Houlton Pioneer Times in 1975–1976—a small sign of how deeply the family was woven into the community's memory of itself.

Guy L. Lenentine died in 1985, in his ninety-second year, at Houlton, Maine—the county seat closest to the New Brunswick countryside his people had come from. (Some records place his death in the early months of 1985 and associate him with Troy, Maine; the precise date and circumstances are not fully resolved in the free sources available here, so the month is given as our family records record it: May 1985.) His life traces, in one person, the larger Lenentine story: a New Brunswick family that crossed the line into Aroostook County and became part of the enduring border community of farms and small towns around Houlton.

A note on sources: the core facts of Guy's birth, his parents, his wife Marguerite, and his death are drawn from our family records and corroborated where possible by free genealogical resources documenting the New Brunswick Lenentines and their move into Maine. Details such as his exact occupation, the full account of his later life, and the deep origin of the surname remain incompletely documented, and have been described here honestly as uncertain rather than invented.
